testingFrequency: Annual for many hazards; every five years for some residences without septic
dueBasis: Leander's hazard list sets frequency by hazard. The FAQ says annual testing applies to fire sprinkler systems, pools with auto-fillers, residences with septic using irrigation, and other listed hazards, while some residences without septic test every five years.
coveredPropertyTypes: Residential properties with fire sprinklers or pools with auto-fillers
Residential irrigation systems, especially where septic is present
coveredDeviceTypes: Grandfathered DCVA in limited older cases
officialListLabel: Use the BSI website for Leander licensed registered testing agencies
penalties: Testing frequency depends on the City's hazard list and grandfather rules; replacement of older grandfathered DCVAs may trigger stricter RPZ requirements.
sourceExcerpt: Leander publishes a hazard-based frequency table, routes new-installation and existing-assembly reports through BSI, and gives unusually clear residential examples in its FAQ.
verdictSummary: Leander is useful because it publishes hazard-based frequency rules, including annual tests for many residential and commercial hazards and five-year testing for some residences without septic.
whoIsAffected: Commercial sites with listed hazards, fire sprinkler systems, pools with auto-fillers, and irrigation systems, especially where septic affects the hazard class.
residentialNotes: Leander is one of the best residential pilot cities because the FAQ explicitly distinguishes annual irrigation cases from five-year low-hazard residential cases.
Pools with auto-fill, fire sprinklers, septic-connected irrigation, and some private-well setups move a property out of the generic low-hazard bucket.
commercialNotes: Commercial sites should treat Leander as hazard-list driven rather than blanket annual-for-everything.
The City also runs a separate commercial irrigation inspection program with a published inspector list, which adds a second workflow next to the backflow assembly rules.

annualTesting.summary: Leander's public materials are unusually specific: many hazards are annual, but some residences without septic are every five years.
highlights: Fire sprinkler systems, pools with auto-fillers, and septic-connected irrigation are annual.
Some residences without septic fall to a five-year testing cycle.
Replacing an old grandfathered DCVA removes the grandfather and requires an RPZ.
workflowSteps: Use the City's hazard examples and FAQ to classify the property.
Schedule the test on the cadence tied to that hazard class.
Route the report through the City's BSI-backed workflow and reevaluate the device type if replacement is needed.

irrigation.summary: Leander's irrigation rules are strong enough to justify a dedicated page. Residential irrigation can be annual or five-year depending on septic and hazard conditions, while commercial irrigation also carries a separate inspection program.
highlights: Residential irrigation with septic requires annual RPZ testing.
Some residences without septic test every five years instead of annually.
Commercial irrigation is tracked through a separate City inspection program with a published qualified-inspector list.
workflowSteps: Determine whether the irrigation system falls into the annual or five-year residential lane.
If the property is commercial, check the separate commercial irrigation inspection program requirements.
Use the qualified tester or inspector list the City points to and submit through the BSI path when the backflow assembly is tested.

fireLine.summary: Leander's FAQ is unusually direct for fire lines: residential and commercial fire sprinkler systems are annual and older grandfathered assemblies lose that status when they are replaced.
highlights: The City treats fire sprinkler systems as a distinct hazard example rather than burying them inside generic commercial copy.
Replacement can force a move from an older grandfathered device to an RPZ.
workflowSteps: Confirm the fire sprinkler assembly's current hazard class and whether a grandfathered device is still allowed.
Schedule the annual fire-system test with the City's approved testing path.
If replacement is needed, confirm whether the device must be upgraded to an RPZ.
Use the Leander hazard list and FAQ to determine the testing frequency for the property.
Use a licensed tester and send new-installation or existing-assembly results through the correct BSI path.
Check whether an older grandfathered DCVA can remain or must be replaced with an RPZ.
Keep the property's hazard conditions current with the City if the use changes.
failureHighlights: Leander's cadence depends heavily on hazard type, so using the wrong local assumption can over- or under-test.
Septic-connected irrigation gets annual RPZ treatment.
A grandfathered DCVA that needs replacement loses its grandfathered status and must be replaced with an RPZ.

costBand.testingRange: Private market pricing varies by device type and whether the hazard class requires annual testing.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair cost widens if a grandfathered assembly now has to be replaced with an RPZ.
costBand.pricingNotes: Leander is one of the better cities for explaining frequency logic, but still not a strong public source for retail pricing.

sources.label: Leander cross-connection and backflow prevention page
